summary: Clear soundboard recording with a historic acoustic opener and standout Dark Star/New Speedway/Lovelight jams.
review: This tape captures one of the first full‑acoustic Dead sets – born from Bill Kreutzmann’s late arrival – followed by an electric second half that builds into a memorable Dark Star, New Speedway Boogie and what many call the best Lovelight of ’69. The soundboard source is clean and well‑balanced, though a few cuts (Rain/Snow and the start of China Cat) are noted. Overall the performance is praised for its historic vibe, strong guitar work from Jerry, soulful organ from Keith/Tom and an inspired acoustic repertoire.

ai_rating.rationale: Over 30 reviews consistently give 4‑5 stars, highlighting exceptional musical moments and a clean soundboard source; minor cuts keep the rating from being higher.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Clean stereo mix with crystal‑clear acoustic guitars and solid organ balance. A few noticeable drop‑outs (Rain/Snow cut, China Cat intro chopped) are the only technical blemishes.

standout_songs: Gathering Flowers for the Master's Bouquet (only performance)
poor_songs: Hard to Handle (mixed reception in one review)
show_quality.setlist_flow: An impromptu acoustic first set (Billy late) gives way to the electric band; energy builds after the second half of Dark Star and peaks with New Speedway Boogie and Lovelight, making the transition a highlight of the show.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Garcia’s 1969 tone described as classic, full‑of color; his acoustic picking and vocal work praised, plus several soaring solos in Dark Star.
band_member_comments.Phil: Bass noted as solid and more audible than on many early tapes; one reviewer says Phil finally shines during the China/DS section.
band_member_comments.Bob: Weir’s (Bobby) acoustic guitar work is clear and his vocal delivery on songs like Little Sadie and Black Peter praised; occasional criticism of his backing vocals on Hard to Handle.
band_member_comments.Keys: Keith Godchaux’s organ highlighted as “tasty” and “fills the spaces”; Tom Constanten’s early organ contributions also receive positive mentions. Pigpen’s organ on Hard to Handle is singled out as exceptional by several reviewers.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill Kreutzmann’s late arrival noted, but once in, his drumming is described as solid throughout the electric set.
